perturbation.original_text: 4.3 Party B shall first pay a deposit of RMB100,000 to Party A within 5 working days after the signing of this Agreement. For list more products, Party B shall apply to Party A 3 working days in advance and after obtaining the consent of Party A, pay the deposit in full within 5 working days from the date of receipt of the deposit notice from Party A.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3 Party B shall first pay a deposit of RMB100,000 to Party A within 1 working day after the signing of this Agreement. For list more products, Party B shall apply to Party A immediately and after obtaining the consent of Party A, pay the deposit in full within 1 working day from the date of receipt of the deposit notice from Party A.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment timeframe to '1 working day' may not provide sufficient time for the lessee to secure funds or transfer the deposit, contradicting fair business practice and regulations regarding reasonable payment periods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of the Civil Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of the General Principles of the Civil Law of the People's Republic of China requires civil activities to be voluntary, fair, and honest. Reducing the deposit payment timeframe to 1 working day may violate fairness if it puts undue pressure on Party B.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: y not compel state governors to surrender fugitives through the issue of writs of mandamus . The Dennison decision was overruled by Puerto Rico v. Branstad (1987); now, the federal courts may require the extradition of fugitives. [ 2 ] Alleged fugitives generally may not challenge extradition proceedings. The motives of the governor demanding the extradition may not be questioned. The accused cannot defend himself against the charges in the extraditing state; the fugitive must do so in the state receiving him. However, the accused may prevent extradition by offering clear evidence that he was not in the state he allegedly fled from at the time of the crime. [ 3 ] There is no constitutional requirement that extradited fugitives be tried only for the crimes named in the extradition proceedings. Fugitives brought to states by means other than extradition may be tried, even though the means of the conveyance was unlawful; the Supreme Court so ruled in Mahon v. Justice , 127 U.S. 700 (1888). In Mahon a body of armed men from Kentucky forcibly took, without a warrant, a man in West Virginia to bring him back to Kentucky for formal arrest and trial. Clause 3: Fugitive Slave Clause [ edit ] Main article: Fugitive Slave Clause No Person held to Service or Labour in one State, under the Laws thereof, escaping into another, shall, in Consequence of any Law or Regulation therein, be discharged from such Service or Labour, but shall be delivered up on Claim of the Party to whom such Service or Labour may be due. Pierce Butler and Charles Pinckney , both from South Carolina , submitted this clause to the Constitutional Convention . James Wilson of Pennsylvania objected, stating it would require that state governments enforce slavery at taxpayers' expense. Butler withdrew the clause. However, on the next day the clause was quietly reinstated and adopted by the Convention without objection. This clause was added to the clause that provided extradition for fugitives from justice. [ 4 ] When first adopted, this clause applied to fugitive slaves and required that they be extradited upon the claims of their masters, but it provided no means for doing so. The Fugitive Slave Act of 1793 created the mechanism for recovering a fugitive slave, overruled any state laws giving sanctuary , made it a federal crime to assist an escaped slave, and allowed slave-catchers into every U.S. state and territory. As free states sought to undermine the federal law, the even more severe Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 was enacted. In 1864, during the Civil War, an effort to repeal this clause of the Constitution failed. The vote in the House was 69 for repeal and 38 against, which was short of the two-to-one vote required to amend the Constitution. [ 5 ] This clause was rendered mostly moot when the Thirteenth Amendment abolished involuntary servitude . Section 3: New states and federal property [ edit ] Clause 1: Admission of new states [ edit ] Main article: Admission to the Union See also: List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union New States may be admitted by the Congress into this Union; but no new States shall be formed or erected within the Jurisdiction of any other State; nor any State be formed by the Junction of two or more States, or parts of States, without the Consent of the Legislatures of the States concerned as well as of the Congress. The First Clause of Section Three, also known as the Admissions Clause , [ 6 ] grants to Congress the authority to admit new states into the Union. Since the establishment of the United States in 1776, the number of states has expanded from the original 13 to 50. It also forbids the creation of new states from parts of existing states without the consent of the affected states legislature and Congress. This latter provision was designed to give Eastern states that still had claims to Western lands (e.g., Virginia and North Carolina ) to have a veto over whether their western counties (which eventually became Kent
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides 5 working days for Party B to pay a deposit, while the changed text reduces this timeframe to 1 working day. The Law Explanation argues this may violate fairness principles under Article 4 of the General Principles of the Civil Law of the People's Republic of China if it puts undue pressure on Party B. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, stating the change may not provide sufficient time, contradicting regulations regarding reasonable payment periods. While the snippets do not directly address payment timeframes, the change falls under the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition because it alters a time-sensitive obligation in a way that may violate a fairness standard, creating an unenforceable or illegal term. The change introduces a potential legal challenge based on insufficient time for compliance, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 19.1 All Platform Rules are an integral part of this Agreement. In the event of any inconsistence between the Platform Rules and this Agreement, the Platform Rules shall prevail. If Party B signs this Agreement and uses the Platform, it shall accept to be bound by the Platform Rules. Party A is entitled to develop and revise the Platform Rules pursuant to the operation of the Platform. For the development and revision of the Platform Rules that may affect Party B's rights and obligations, Party A will notify Party B in writing via email 10 days prior to the implementation of the new Platform Rules, and Party B shall decide whether to continue to perform this Agreement within 10 days from the date of receipt of the notice via email. If Party B rejects the new Platform Rules, it shall send a written application for the termination of this Agreement to Party A within 10 days from the date of receipt of such notice. If Party B fails to terminate this Agreement or continue to log in and use the Platform within such 10-day period, it shall be deemed to agree to the new Platform Rules. The new Platform Rules shall become effective as of the effective date specified in the Platform Rules, and Party B shall strictly abide by the new Platform Rules as of the effective date.
perturbation.changed_text: 19.1 All Platform Rules are an integral part of this Agreement. In the event of any inconsistence between the Platform Rules and this Agreement, the Platform Rules shall prevail. If Party B signs this Agreement and uses the Platform, it shall accept to be bound by the Platform Rules. Party A is entitled to develop and revise the Platform Rules pursuant to the operation of the Platform. For the development and revision of the Platform Rules that may affect Party B's rights and obligations, Party A will notify Party B in writing via email 2 days prior to the implementation of the new Platform Rules, and Party B shall decide whether to continue to perform this Agreement within 2 days from the date of receipt of the notice via email. If Party B rejects the new Platform Rules, it shall send a written application for the termination of this Agreement to Party A within 2 days from the date of receipt of such notice. If Party B fails to terminate this Agreement or continue to log in and use the Platform within such 2-day period, it shall be deemed to agree to the new Platform Rules. The new Platform Rules shall become effective as of the effective date specified in the Platform Rules, and Party B shall strictly abide by the new Platform Rules as of the effective date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period to 2 days does not allow Party B sufficient time to assess the impact of the rule changes and potentially violates principles of fair dealing and reasonable notice periods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 7 mandates that parties should adhere to the principle of good faith in performing their contractual obligations. A 2-day notice period may be deemed insufficient for Party B to adequately assess and respond to changes in Platform Rules, especially if those changes have a significant impact, thus violating the 'good faith' requirement.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period from 10 days to 2 days, which contradicts the principle of good faith as it does not allow Party B sufficient time to assess and respond to changes in Platform Rules. The Law Explanation states that Article 7 mandates parties should adhere to the principle of good faith. The shortened notice period undermines Party B's ability to make an informed decision, failing the good faith requirement. While U.C.C. - ARTICLE 7 - DOCUMENTS OF TITLE, doesn't explicitly state good faith, the general concept of fair dealing in contractual obligations means providing reasonable notice. Therefore, a contradiction exists.
